note: Recursion/Backtracking, DP, Graphs, Trees, Sorting & Searching, Stack, Greedy — Batch 2 of 100-problem bank

problems.title: N-Queens — Count Distinct Solutions
problems.description: Place `n` queens on an `n×n` chessboard so that no two share a row, column, or diagonal. Count **how many** distinct full placements exist (rotations/reflections count separately).
problems.inputFormat: One integer `n` (1 ≤ n ≤ 9).
problems.outputFormat: One integer — the number of valid queen placements.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ n ≤ 9. Answer fits in 32-bit signed int.
hints: Place queens row by row; track occupied columns and both diagonal directions.
Use sets or bitmasks for O(1) conflict checks when n is small.
Prune as soon as a row has no legal column — classic backtracking.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){int n; cin>>n; return 0;}

problems.title: Well-Formed Parentheses — Count (Catalan)
problems.description: For a given `n`, how many **distinct** strings of `n` pairs of parentheses are **balanced** (well-formed)? This is the `n`th Catalan number.
problems.inputFormat: One integer `n` (0 ≤ n ≤ 12). Use Catalan(0)=1 (empty string).
problems.outputFormat: One integer — number of valid length-`2n` parenthesis strings.
hints: Catalan satisfies C(n+1) = sum_{i=0..n} C(i)*C(n-i).
Or: first return to balance splits the string into two independent balanced parts.
Dynamic programming on n avoids recomputing smaller Catalan values.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){int n; cin>>n; return 0;}

problems.title: Word Search on a Grid
problems.description: Given a character grid and a word, determine if the word can be built by moving **up/down/left/right** from cell to cell without reusing a cell in the same search. Each step must match the next character.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `R C`. Next `R` lines: `C` characters each (letters A–Z or a–z). Last line: the word (non-empty).
problems.outputFormat: Print `YES` if the word exists, else `NO`.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ R,C ≤ 6, word length ≤ 15. Grid and word use ASCII letters only.
hints: DFS from each cell that matches the first letter; mark visited, then unmark on backtrack.
Prune when the next character has no unvisited neighbor.
Grid is tiny — recursion depth is bounded by word length.

problems.title: Minimum Path Sum in a Grid
problems.description: You start at the top-left of a grid of non-negative costs and may only move **right** or **down**. Minimize the sum of cells on a path to the bottom-right.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `M N`. Next `M` lines: `N` non-negative integers each.
problems.outputFormat: One integer — minimum path sum.
hints: dp[i][j] = cell[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
Initialize first row and first column as prefix sums along edges.
O(MN) time, O(min(M,N)) space possible with rolling array.

problems.title: Edit Distance (Levenshtein)
problems.description: Given two strings, find the minimum number of single-character **insertions**, **deletions**, or **substitutions** to turn the first string into the second.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: string A. Line 2: string B (lowercase letters, may be empty).
problems.outputFormat: One integer — minimum operations.
hints: dp[i][j] = cost to convert A[0..i) to B[0..j).
If A[i-1]==B[j-1], dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]; else 1 + min of three neighbors.
Base cases: empty string requires |other| insertions or deletions.

problems.title: Longest Palindromic Subsequence Length
problems.description: Return the length of the **longest subsequence** of `s` that reads the same forwards and backwards (not necessarily contiguous).
problems.inputFormat: One line: string `s` (lowercase letters).
problems.outputFormat: One integer — LPS length.
hints: For interval [i,j], if s[i]==s[j] then length is 2 + answer for (i+1,j-1), else max of (i+1,j) and (i,j-1).
Fill DP by increasing length of substring.
Equivalent to LCS(s, reverse(s)).

problems.title: Palindrome Partitioning II — Minimum Cuts
problems.description: Cut string `s` into palindrome substrings. What is the **minimum number of cuts** in the gaps between characters? A string of length 1 needs **0** cuts.
problems.inputFormat: One line: string `s` (lowercase letters).
problems.outputFormat: One integer — minimum cuts.
hints: First precompute isPalindrome[i][j] for all intervals.
dp[j] = min cuts for prefix ending at j; try every palindrome ending at j.
O(n^3) is OK for n=500 with careful constant factor; O(n^2) possible with optimized transition.

problems.title: Target Sum — Number of Assignments
problems.description: You have an array of **positive** integers. Place `+` or `-` before each so the expression equals `target`. Count how many **different** sign assignments work.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n` and `target`. Line 2: `n` positive integers.
problems.outputFormat: One integer — number of ways (fits 32-bit for given limits).
hints: Let S = sum(nums). You need subset P with 2P - S = target, i.e. P = (S+target)/2.
If (S+target) is odd or negative P is impossible, answer 0.
Classic bounded/unbounded-style knapsack counting on subset sum P.

problems.title: Dungeon Game — Minimum Starting HP
problems.description: A knight crosses an `M×N` dungeon. Each cell changes HP (negative = damage, positive = orb). He needs **at least 1** HP at every moment, including exit. From `(0,0)` moving only right/down, what is the **minimum initial HP** at start?
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `M N`. Next `M` lines: `N` integers (each in [-1000,1000]).
problems.outputFormat: One positive integer — minimum starting HP.
hints: Work backwards from the princess cell: need at least 1 HP after the last cell.
dp[i][j] = min HP entering (i,j) to survive to the end.
Transition: after cell value, HP must stay ≥ 1 when stepping to the better of right/down.

problems.title: Maximal Square of Ones
problems.description: Given a binary matrix (`0`/`1`), find the **area** (number of cells) of the largest square containing only `1`s.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `M N`. Next `M` lines: `N` integers 0 or 1.
problems.outputFormat: One integer — area of largest all-1 square.
hints: dp[i][j] = side length of largest square ending at (i,j) if matrix[i][j]==1.
Recurrence: 1 + min of three neighbors when cell is 1.
Track max side, answer is maxSide^2.

problems.title: Is Graph Bipartite?
problems.description: Undirected simple graph on nodes `0..n-1`. Determine whether vertices can be **2-colored** so every edge connects different colors.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n m`. Next `m` lines: `u v` (0-based, undirected, no self-loops).
problems.outputFormat: `YES` if bipartite, else `NO`.
hints: BFS/DFS coloring; on conflict, not bipartite.
Graph may be disconnected — try every component.
Self-loops are excluded; parallel edges can be ignored or treated as one.

problems.title: Undirected Graph — Cycle Detection
problems.description: Simple undirected graph on `0..n-1`. Print `YES` if **any** cycle exists, `NO` if the graph is a forest (acyclic).
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n m`. Next `m` lines: `u v` (0-based).
problems.outputFormat: `YES` or `NO`.
hints: DFS: if you reach a visited neighbor that is not the parent, you found a cycle.
Alternatively: in each tree component, edges = nodes - 1; extra edge implies cycle.
Handle isolated vertices and multiple components.

problems.title: Connected Components (Undirected)
problems.description: Count the number of **connected components** in an undirected graph on nodes `0..n-1`.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n m`. Next `m` lines: `u v`.
problems.outputFormat: One integer — component count.
hints: Union-Find or DFS from unvisited nodes; each DFS start is a new component.
Isolated vertices are their own components.
Parallel edges do not change connectivity.

problems.title: Is the Graph a Tree?
problems.description: A **tree** on `n` labeled nodes is connected and has exactly `n-1` edges. Given an undirected simple graph, decide if it is a tree.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n m`. Next `m` lines: `u v` (0-based).
problems.outputFormat: `YES` if the graph is a tree, else `NO`.
hints: Necessary: m = n-1 and graph is connected (for n≥1).
If m ≠ n-1, it cannot be a tree (unless n=1 and m=0 special case).
Use BFS/DFS from 0 to verify connectivity when m = n-1.

problems.title: Tree Diameter (Unweighted)
problems.description: Given a **tree** with `n` nodes and `n-1` undirected edges, the **diameter** is the maximum distance (in edges) between any two nodes. Output that diameter.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n`. Next `n-1` lines: `u v` (0-based).
problems.outputFormat: One integer — diameter length (number of edges on longest path).
hints: Two BFS/DFS: from any node find farthest A; from A find farthest distance D.
That D is the diameter (standard property of trees).
O(n) time.

problems.title: Aggressive Cows (Maximize Minimum Distance)
problems.description: `n` stalls lie on a number line at given coordinates (unsorted). Place `c` cows in stalls so the **minimum distance** between any two cows is as large as possible. Output that maximum achievable minimum distance.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n c`. Line 2: `n` distinct integers — stall positions (0 ≤ pos ≤ 10^9).
problems.outputFormat: One integer — maximum minimum spacing between cows.
hints: Sort stalls; binary search the answer `d`: can we place all cows with pairwise distance ≥ d?
Greedy placement: put first cow in leftmost stall, then next cow in earliest stall at least `d` away.
Feasibility check is O(n) for fixed d.

problems.title: Koko Eating Bananas
problems.description: Koko has `n` piles of bananas; pile `i` has `piles[i]` bananas. Guards return in `H` hours. Each hour she chooses one pile and eats `k` bananas from it (or all remaining if fewer). What is the **minimum integer `k`** per hour so she finishes all piles within `H` hours?
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n H`. Line 2: `n` positive integers — pile sizes.
problems.outputFormat: One integer — minimum eating speed `k`.
hints: If `k` works, any larger `k` also works — monotonic predicate.
Binary search `k` from 1 to max(piles).
Hours needed for pile x is ceil(x/k); sum must be ≤ H.

problems.title: Find Peak Index (Strict Peak)
problems.description: Array `nums` length `n`. Imagine `nums[-1] = nums[n] = -∞`. Return the **smallest** index `i` such that `nums[i] > nums[i-1]` and `nums[i] > nums[i+1]`. A **strict** peak always exists under the given constraints.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n`. Line 2: `n` integers — strictly positive values with at least one strict interior peak.
problems.outputFormat: One integer — smallest valid peak index (0-based).
problems.constraints: 3 ≤ n ≤ 10^5, 1 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 10^9. Test data guarantees a strict peak exists.
hints: Scan left to right and return the first index where the value is greater than both neighbors.
Binary search also works: compare mid with mid+1 to shrink toward a peak.
Sentinel -∞ at the ends is conceptual only — it is not in the input.

problems.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
problems.description: Given non-negative bar heights in order, find the **maximum area** of a rectangle aligned to the x-axis that fits under the histogram.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n`. Line 2: `n` non-negative integers — heights.
problems.outputFormat: One integer — maximum rectangle area.
hints: Monotonic stack of indices with increasing heights.
When popping, compute area with popped bar as shortest height.
Append 0 height at end to flush the stack.

problems.title: Jump Game II — Minimum Jumps
problems.description: Array `nums` of non-negative integers; from index `i` you may jump at most `nums[i]` steps forward. Start at index `0`. Reach the last index in the **minimum number of jumps**. It is guaranteed that the last index is reachable.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n`. Line 2: `n` non-negative integers.
problems.outputFormat: One integer — minimum jumps.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ n ≤ 10^4, 0 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 1000. Last index always reachable.
hints: Greedy layer BFS: extend farthest reachable with current jump budget.
When you exhaust the current layer, increment jumps and start next layer.
O(n) single pass.
